student_health_section.medication_allergy: Amoxicillin (develops hives and facial swelling; reaction first noted at age 4, confirmed by pediatrician).
student_health_section.allergic_to: Seasonal environmental allergens (primarily tree pollen in spring and ragweed in late summer/fall). Reactions include sneezing, watery/itchy eyes, and mild nasal congestion. No history of anaphylaxis to foods or insect stings. Also has a documented allergy to amoxicillin and related penicillin-based antibiotics, which causes hives and facial swelling; this is listed in his pediatric records.
student_health_section.other_specify: Mild exercise-induced asthma and seasonal allergic rhinitis. Asthma is generally well-controlled with preventive medication at home and a rescue inhaler as needed. Triggers include extended outdoor activity in cold air, high pollen days, and intense running without adequate warm-up. No history of hospitalization or ICU admission for asthma, and no history of wheezing requiring oral steroids in the past two years. Ethan and both parents have been educated on proper inhaler technique and recognize early warning signs (tightness in chest, difficulty catching breath after running, and persistent coughing). He occasionally experiences mild nosebleeds in very dry winter weather, which resolve with brief pressure and have never required medical intervention.
student_health_section.medications_taken_at_home:
1) Fluticasone propionate (Flovent HFA) 44 mcg: 2 puffs by inhalation twice daily using a spacer, prescribed by Dr. Karen Li (pediatric pulmonologist) for maintenance of mild persistent asthma. Parents monitor adherence and typically administer in the morning before school and at bedtime.
2) Cetirizine (Zyrtec) 10 mg: 1 tablet by mouth once daily during peak allergy seasons (spring and fall) to manage seasonal allergic rhinitis symptoms such as sneezing, itchy/watery eyes, and runny nose.
3) Children’s multivitamin chewable: 1 tablet by mouth daily with breakfast. No other routine prescription medications taken at home.
student_health_section.medications_to_be_given_at_school: Albuterol sulfate inhaler (ProAir HFA) 90 mcg: 2 puffs by inhalation via spacer every 4 hours as needed for wheezing, chest tightness, or shortness of breath, and 15–20 minutes prior to strenuous physical activity (e.g., running in PE class or outdoor recess on high-pollen or very cold days) per asthma action plan on file from Dr. Karen Li. Ethan is familiar with using his inhaler but should be supervised by school staff to ensure correct technique and appropriate timing of doses.

part_i_to_grant_consent.doctor: Emily J. Roberts, MD (Pediatrician, Dayton Children’s Pediatrics – Centerville)
part_i_to_grant_consent.dentist: Mark A. Jensen, DDS (Centerville Family Dentistry)
part_i_to_grant_consent.medical_specialist: Karen S. Li, MD (Pediatric Pulmonology & Allergy, Dayton Children’s Hospital)
